coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of optically transparent and photoluminescent electrospun nanofiber composed of carbon quantum dots and polyacrylonitrile blend with polyacrylic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Alam, Al-Mahmnur
Liu, Yanan
Park, Mira
Park, Soo-Jin
Kim, Hak-Yong
coredata.dc:description: Novel photoluminescence (PL) composite nanofibers (NFs) consist of carbon qunatum dots (CQDs) and polyacrylonitrile blend with polyacrylic acid (PAN/PAA) were fabricated by the coelectrospinning process. The air-dried CQDs containing NFs were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-visible spectroscopy, spectrofluorometer, and confocal microscopy. CQDs are familiar to emit blue, green and red color depending on the excitation energy. The PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs were found to be optically transparent and exhibited PL properties similar to CQDs. Therefore, blue, green and red color was observed under confocal microscope from the NFs membrane while the sample was excited by 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm lasers. The results indicated the well preserved quantum confinement properties of the CQDs inside the polymer matrix without aggregation or substantial quantum dots growth.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Carbon quantum dots
Polyacrylonitrile

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon QDs
Optical behavior of the composite nanofibers

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean government (No. 2014R1A4A1008140 ), Ministry of Education, Science Technology (MEST) and National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) through the Human Resource Training Project for Regional Innovation (No. 201210A0404613010100 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of (a) PAN/PAA Nanofiber, (b) PAN/PAA/CQD-10 nanofiber composite and (c) PAN/PAA/CQD-30 nanofiber composite.
TEM images of PAN/PAA Nanofiber at (a) low and (b) high magnification; PAN/PAA/CQD-10 nanofiber composite at (c) low and (d) high and PAN/PAA/CQD-30 at (e) low and (f) high.
FTIR spectra of (a) CQDs; (b) PAN/PAA; (c) PAN/PAA/CQD-10 and (d) PANN/PAA/CQD-30.
XPS survey spectra (A) and High resolution N1s spectrum of (B) CQDs; (C) PAN/PAA NFs and (D) PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs.
PL emission spectra of PAN/PAA(s); CQDs in solution (a, d and g); PAN/PAA/CQD-10 (b, e and g) and PAN/PAA/CQD-30 (c, f and i).

Confocal microscopic images of (a) CQD solution; (b) PAN/PAA nanofiber without composited with CQD; (c) PAN/PAA/CQD-30 composite (low magnification) and (d) PAN/PAA/CQD-30 composite (high magnification).
UV-vis Transmittance spectra of (a) PAN/PAA; (b) PAN/PAA/CQD-10 and (c) PAN/PAA/CQD-30.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of optically transparent and photoluminescent electrospun nanofiber composed of carbon quantum dots and polyacrylonitrile blend with polyacrylic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of BIN Fusion Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Materials and Fiber Enginering, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic Materials and Fiber Enginering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100-Inharo, Incheon 402-751, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of BIN Fusion Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, South Korea. Tel.: +82 63 270 2351; fax: +82 63 270 4249.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel photoluminescence (PL) composite nanofibers (NFs) consist of carbon qunatum dots (CQDs) and polyacrylonitrile blend with polyacrylic acid (PAN/PAA) were fabricated by the coelectrospinning process. The air-dried CQDs containing NFs were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-visible spectroscopy, spectrofluorometer, and confocal microscopy. CQDs are familiar to emit blue, green and red color depending on the excitation energy. The PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs were found to be optically transparent and exhibited PL properties similar to CQDs. Therefore, blue, green and red color was observed under confocal microscope from the NFs membrane while the sample was excited by 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm lasers. The results indicated the well preserved quantum confinement properties of the CQDs inside the polymer matrix without aggregation or substantial quantum dots growth.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), as nanocrystals, possess tunable optical and electric properties, photochemical stability, and have found wide applications in areas such as energy and optical sensing, optical amplification, and fluorescent labeling [1,2]. Polymer nanocomposites with nanomaterials specially QDs have appealed much concentration because of their unique physicochemical properties and wide potential applications in the field of electronic and optical devices, sensors, electrode materials, smart clothing, membranes, and wound-dressing materials, etc. [3–5]. In fact, the systematic introduction of nanomaterials such as QDs, magnetic nanoparticles, photocatalytic nanoparticles or carbon nanotube in the polymer matrix will not only generate new composite with excellent physiochemical properties but also helps to avoid the aggregation of the particles inside the matrix [6,7]. It is because of QDs capability to be well-dispersed throughout the polymer nanofibers (NFs) without apparent aggregation within similar solvent environment. Thus Förster resonance energy transfer between neighboring QDs did not occur and the fluorescence properties of the single QDs are preserved [8]. Electrospining has emerged as a versatile technique capable of processing rich variety of ultra-thin fiber with diameters down to 50 nm and lengths up to a kilometer, providing a straightforward means to produce NFs composite from polymers and QDs or nanoparticles for target application [9].

To date, intense attention has been focused to design and characterize a variety of QDs/polymer nanocomposite such as ZnO/PVA, CdS/PVP, ZnO/poly(vinyl pyrrolidone), CdTe/PVA, CdSe–ZnS/PVA, CdSe–ZnS/PS, TiO2/PVP, TiO2/PPV for optical sensing or catalytic sensing [10–16]. The widely synthesized and used semiconductor QDs derived from metallic elements, such as CdS, CdSe, PbSe, CdSe/ZnS, CdSe/CdS etc are environmentally and biologically hazardous which limits their practical application. But carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new member of the nanocarbon family have been originated to be enthralling QDs for numerous applications owing to their favorable tunable luminescent properties, high chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and easy functionalization [17]. CQDs display spectrally broad (unstructured) excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) emission but the origins of PL are not yet entirely understood. The multicolor PL emission of CQDs may arise from the radiative recombination of excitons of carbon, emissive traps, quantum-confinement effect, aromatic structures/oxygen-containing groups, free zig-zag sites or edge defects [18]. In recent years, CQDs have been demonstrated to possess high emission quantum yields up to 60–80% [19]. The multicolor PL emission property of CQD has been used to design excellent opto-electronic devices, fluorescence bio-imaging probes or security-check devices [20–22]. It is already used for making color switchable light emitting diode based on changing the voltage parameter of the devices [19]. There are a lot of reports already available for nanofiber prepared from many different polymer and QDs [10–16]. However, there is no report yet for transparent nanofiber composed of CQDs and polymer specially polyacrylonitrile blend with polyacrylic acid (PAN/PAA).

In the present report, we have described the fabrication of PAN/PAA/CQDs composite nanofibers (NFs) membrane by coelectrospining process and focused on the optical behavior specially PL properties of the composite at room temperature. The composite NFs were found to be exhibited blue, green, and red color emission similar to that of the CQDs when they were excited with different excitation energy. The results implied the well preserved property of CQDs inside the polymer matrix along with throughout distribution inside the fibers. Therefore, the stemmed composite NFs by the co-electrospinning has shaped a new pathway to transfer special nanoscale effect to macro structure performance in various application fields such as anti-counterfeiting, UV protector, multi-functional textile, optoelectronic devices, biological labeling, and damage detection for composite structures.

PAN and PAA with average molecular weight of 150,000 g/mol and 250,000 g/mol respectively (Sigma–Aldrich) were used as the polymer matrix and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%, Sigma–Aldrich) was employed as the solvent for dissolving PAN and PAA. Urea (99.9%, Sigma–Aldrich), Citric acid (99.5%, Sigma–Aldrich), Ethanol (99.9%, Sigma–Aldrich) was employed for synthesis of CQDs. All the chemicals were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon QDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized using microwave assisted pyrolysis method in accordance with the procedure reported elsewhere [23] with some modification. In brief, 1 g citric acid (CA) and 0.5 g of urea was dissolved in a 50 mL vial using 10 mL deionized water before microwave (700W) treatment for 5 min. The obtained dark brown clustered solid was dissolve in ethanol for extracting CQDs. The solution containing CQDs was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove large or agglomerated particle. The resulting solution was kept overnight in an oven at 80C. Finally the black dried sample was collected, measured and dispersed in DMSO. The brownish solution having concentration 10 and 30 mg/mL was used for further experiment. The as prepared CQDs were identified and characterized by UV-vis spectroscopy (Fig.S-1), transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images and (PL) spectra (Fig.S-2) as shown in supplementary information (SI). The observed information was identical with the reported results [23,24].

In a typical experimental procedure, transparent PAN/PAA/CQDs composite NFs were prepared with five steps: (1) Preparation of composite PAN/PAA solutions (DMSO, 7wt%) at weight ratio 1:1; (2) Dilution of the colloidal solution of CQDs to desire volume in DMSO; (3) Preparation of electrospinning precursor solution using 8g PAN/PAA solutions with 10 mg/mL (CQD-10) and 30 mg/mL (CQD-30) of CQDs colloidal solution; (5) Fabrication of composite transparent PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs by electrospinning at room temperature with 75% relative humidity. The applied voltage was 15 KV and the distance between the needle tip and the collector was 15 cm. The feeding rate of the solution was 1 mL/h. Finally, the composite NFs were dried at room temperature in relatively closed environment.

The surface morphology of NFs was studied by using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-7400, Japan) operated at 1.00 kV electron. TEM images were captured by using Philips Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (Philips, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of samples was recorded on a BRUKER VECTOR-22 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission measurements were performed using LS50B Luminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). UV-Vis absorption and transmittance was measured on a TU-1810 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, China). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha KA1066 spectrometer using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source (hʋ = 1486.6 eV). Photoluminescence life time decay profile was measured using Time Correlated Single Photon Counting Spectrometer (FLS920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

The FE-SEM and TEM images of the electrospun NFs in absence and presence of CQD-10 and CQD-30 were presented in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 respectively. The surface of the elcetrospun PAN/PAA NFs with and without CQDs was not smooth as seen in the FE-SEM images illustrated in Fig. 1. It was also seen that fiber morphology of PAN/PAA NFs in absence and presence of CQDs was not changed apparently.

On the other hand, the presence of CQDs in the composite NFs was confirmed by the TEM images. The corresponding TEM images of PAN/PAA NFs in absence and presence of CQD-10 and CQD-30 was demonstrated in low and high resolution in Fig. 2. The rough surface of the NFs was clearly observed in all cases as shown in Fig. 2. The diameter of the as prepared NFs can be calculated from the scale bar around 300–400 nm. The presence of CQDs in composite NFs prepares from both CQD-10 and CQD-30 was identified inside the fiber and recorded in low and high magnification as shown in Fig. 2 (c-low, and d-high) and Fig. 2 (e-low and d-high) respectively. The CQDs were found to be uniformly distributed inside the fiber structure as shown in both low and high resolution images without aggregation. Higher numbers of CQDs were observed in the composite from CQD-30 (Fig. 2e and f) in comparison with that of the CQD-10 (Fig. 2c and d) as shown in the Fig. 2. The average size of the CQDs used in this study was about 3–5 nm, as demonstrates in the TEM images in Fig. S-2(a).

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed in order to investigate the surface functional group of CQDs and their interaction with the polymer in the matrix by comparing the FTIR spectra of the composite PAN/PAA NFs with and without CQDs. According to Fig. 3 a, CQDs showed broad absorption band at 3100–3500 cm−1 which is attributed to O–H and N–H group. Absorption bands at 1617–1770 cm−1 are assigned to CO group indicates the surface carboxylic group [23]. Absorption band at 1570 cm−1 indicated the amide (CONH) bending. Therefore, the presence of surface OH–/COO– group has increased the dispersibility of CQDs in polar organic or aqueous media. This property of CQDs was advantageous to prepare homogeneous electrospining polymer solution blend with PAN/PAA in DMSO at room temperature.

The FTIR spectrum of PAN/PAA NFs was illustrated in Fig 3b. The strong absorption band located at 3471 cm−1 is assigned to OH group present in PAA. Absorption band at 2241 cm−1 is the characteristic nitrile (CN) bond stretching of PAN and strong absorption band at 1710 cm−1 assigned to the carboxylic group (COO− asymmetric stretching) of PAA in the PAN/PAA composite NFs before introducing CQDs. The result clearly indicated that PAN and PAA were chemically bonded through available functional group to host suitable foreign particles. Besides, a series of characteristic bands in the regions 2924 cm−1, 1451 cm−1, 1251 cm−1, 1006 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of different modes in methylene group of PAN [24].

The interaction of CQD-10 and CQD-30 with PAN/PAA fiber was attempted to figure out through the spectral change in Fig. 3b–d for PAN/PAA, PAN/PAA/CQD-10 and PAN/PA/CQD-30 respectively. The absorption band of PAN/PAA at 1710 cm−1 was found to be shifted to 1705 cm−1 for CQD-30 and unchanged for CQD-10 indicating the concentration effect of CQDs to be interacted in the PAN/PAA matrix. The most importantly, a new absorption band appeared at 1548 cm−1 (Fig. 3c) and 1558 cm−1 (Fig. 3d) for PAN/PAA/CQD-10 and PAN/PA/CQD-30 respectively which can be assigned to the bending vibrations of the amide band (CONH) and this was observed for CQDs in Fig. 3(a). The band might be associated with stretching vibrations of C–N. This result implied the interaction between CQDs and PAN/PAA NFs. It might be happened between the available surface N–H group in CQDs and available carboxylic (COO−) group in PAN/PAA fiber. Thus carboxylic groups mainly available from PAA might also have contributed to stabilize the CQDs without further aggregation in the matrix. It is already reported that hydrophilic polymers such as PAA can be used to stabilize nanoparticles through an ester bond between the carboxyl group of the hydrophilic polymer and the hydroxyl group of nanoparticle [25]. Based on this discussion it was assumed that the CQDs were distributed uniformly in solution and expected to be stabilized by PAA in PAN/PAA blend solution. Therefore, the fast evaporation rate of the solvent in the electrospinning process made the polymer chains quickly frozen and the CQDs confined to the polymer chains with no time to aggregate leading to the good conservancy of the original PL properties of the CQDs [26]. Therefore, the multicolor PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs were prepared through electrospinning.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed to investigate the content of nitrogen atom of the CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the XPS survey spectra of CQDs, PAN/PAA and PAN/PAA/CQDs show predominant N1s peak at ca. 399 eV, graphitic C1s peak at ca. 284 eV and O1s peak at ca. 531 eV. A pronounced N 1s peak was observed for all samples. The high resolution N1s spectrum of the CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs reveals the presence of pyridine-like and pyrrolic/amide and quaternary nitrogen atom [27]. The atomic percentage of nitrogen calculated from XPS data in the as-obtained CQDs, PAN/PAA NFs and PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs is 11.87%, 3.01% and 7.93% respectively. The higher content of nitrogen in the PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs composite than that of the PAN/PAA NFs indicated the presence of nitrogen containing CQDs in the polymer matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical behavior of the composite nanofibers
ce:section.ce:para: According to reported data as prepared CQDs possess excitation dependent PL emissions. Thus CQDs-polymer composited nanofiber is also expected to exhibit the same characteristic PL performance. The excitation dependent PL emission spectra of the CQDs colloidal solution in DMSO were illustrated in Fig. 5 which is similar to the reported results [23,24]. Three emission spectra was observed at the wavelength of 442 nm, 522 nm and 580 nm corresponding to blue, green and red emission from same sample solution when it was excited at the wavelength of 380 nm, 480 nm and 545 nm respectively as shown in the Fig. S-2. On the other hand, CQDs shows bright blue emission under UV lamp (365 nm) as shown in the Fig. S-1 (inset).

The PL emission spectra of 10 times diluted PAN/PAA (by DMSO) electrospinning NFs solution in absence and presence of CQDs was demonstrated in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5 (s), no observable emission within visible spectrum range was found from the electrospining solution in absence of CQDs when it was excited at the wavelength of 380 nm, 480 nm or 545 nm. However, PAN/PAA/CQD-10 emitted light at the wavelength of 444 nm, 523 nm and 580 nm as shown in Fig. 5b, e and h respectively.

On the other hand, PAN/PAA/CQD-30 emitted light at the wavelength of 446 nm, 530 nm and 580 nm as shown in Fig. 5b, e and h respectively. In both cases, little red shifting from original CQDs emission wavelength was observed which might be resulted from the interaction of CQDs and polymer functional group in the matrix. The corresponding excitation spectra of CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQDs were also recorded as shown in Fig. S-3. The obtained excitation spectra of CQDs were peaked at 375 nm, 465 nm and 541 nm when the sample was excited at their corresponding emission wavelength at 442 nm, 522 nm and 580 nm. For PAN/PAA/CQDs, the excitation spectra were found to be emerged in similar fashion and peaked at 376.5 nm, 478 nm and 550 nm upon excitation at the emission wavelength of 446 nm, 530 nm and 580 nm.

It was interesting to observe that PL signal was found to be enhanced noticeably with little red shifting from composite elecstrospining solution while throughout scanning at the wavelength of 380 nm, 480 nm or 545 nm comparing with the emission only from CQDs solution (Fig. 5a, d and g). The reason can be explained as following. It was already reported that nitrogen containing CQDs, selected for this work, exhibit excitation-dependent and high PL performance [23]. The doped nitrogen has played vital role to enhance the emission of the CQDs through upward shifting of the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [28]. Moreover, the nitrogen bonding to carbon creates emission energy traps for the particles through the radiative recombination induced by electron–hole pairs. The reason of emission can be derived from direct recombination of the excited electrons from the n→π* transitions of the surface CO and CN groups. As prepared CQDs are already nitrogen doped as shown in FTIR spectra depicted in Fig. 3(a). The PAN/PAA polymer matrix also contains nitrogen. Thus the further enhancement of emission in PAN/PAA/CQDs composite might be resulted from the interaction between polymer nitrogen with surface functional group in CQDs in the matrix. The emission peaks of electrospining solution did not shift too much compared to pure CQDs solution suggesting excellent individual dispersion of CQDs into PAN/PAA solution. The increase in the concentration of the CQDs in the solution also did not affect the PL peak positions vigorously, but enhances in the intensity of the PL emission peaks. This specified that there was no substantial QDs growth or aggregation at all concentrations during the electrospinning process and thus this proves good quantum confinement properties of the QDs. It was evident from the TEM images that QDs are evenly located inside the matrix.

It was also observed that PAN/PAA NFs did not emit blue emission in absence of CQDs under UV lamp (Fig.S–4B-b). However, the spectacular blue color emission was seen through naked eye from the composite PAN/PAA NFs in presence of CQD-10 and CQD-30 as depicted in Fig. S–4B(a) and Fig. S–4B(c) respectively. Individual confocal microscopic image of CQDs solution in DMSO and air dried PAN/PAA/CQDs composite NFs membrane was also captured by exciting the same sample with three different lasers in different magnification as shown in Fig. 6 . Blue, green and red color emission was identified and recorded from the same CQDs sample solution when it was excited at 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm laser as shown in Fig. 6a. This result demonstrated the consistency with already recorded the PL emission spectra in Fig. 5a, d and g of the CQDs solution.

There was not found any color to display in PAN/PAA NFs in absence of CQDs shown in Fig. 6b. On the other hand blue, green and red color was detected and recorded in low (Fig. 6c) and high (Fig. 6d) magnification from air dried PAN/PAA/CQDs when the sample was laser-excited at the wavelength of 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm respectively. The individual fiber was also found to display uniformly blue, green and red color as shown in highly magnified image in Fig. 6d. Therefore, the uniformity of the color emissions throughout the electrospun composite PAN/PAA/CQD NFs confirms the uniform distribution of CQDs without aggregation in the polymer solid matrix. The characteristic PL behavior of CQDs was well preserved inside the transparent PAN/PAA NFs.

Another important feature of the described NFs membrane was transparency. The transparency of three kinds of NFs membrane was illustrated in Fig. S-4 under daylight and UV light. The air dried PAN/PAA NFs was found to exhibit upto 65% transparency in the range of UV to visible wavelength as shown in Fig. 7 a. On the contrary, transparency was reduced to 60 and 49% for PAN/PAA/CQD-10 (Fig. 7b) and PAN/PAA/CQD-30 (Fig. 7c) respectively. The concentration of CQDs was responsible for the reduction of transparency. Both kinds of composite NFs can transmit visible light in the wavelength range from 430 to 800 nm while absorbing the part of the UV spectrum as shown in Fig. 7b and c. This might be resulted from the CQDs present in the polymer matrix. But the absorption capability of PAN/PAA/CQD-30 was little higher than that of PAN/PAA/CQD-10. This property of the as prepared CQDs containing NFs membrane can be utilized to harvest UV shielding materials upon further optimized research.

PL quantum yield, life time of CQDs and life time of PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs was also determined. The quantum yield of the as-obtained CQDs is about 23%, comparable to the previously reported value [23]. Quinine sulfate was used as standard reference material for determination of quantum yield of CQDs in solution. The emission and absorption spectra of quinine sulfate and CQDs and details of the quantum yield determination are provided in the in Fig. S5. The PL decay profile of the CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs membrane was obtained by using a time correlated single photon counting technique with recording the transitions at 440 nm and 450 nm emission for CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQD NFs respectively upon excitation at 375 nm laser diode. The PL life time data are well fitted as described in Fig. S6 to an exponential function and indicating that the observed life time (t) is 7.21 ns and 5.15 ns for CQDs and PAN/PAA/CQDs NFs respectively.

Electrospun PAN/PAA/CQDs composite NFs were successfully prepared by incorporating CQDs in the pre-electrospinning PAN/PAA polymer solution. It was demonstrated that CQDs were uniformly distributed inside the polymer matrix without sacrificing their optical properties as well as without disturbing the morphology of NFs too. Therefore, CQDs were found to successful conversion of NFs from non-luminescent state to multicolor emission to display blue, green and red color upon excitation by different excitation energy. These results will lead to develop a new family of advanced product of carbon based QDs and polymer such as anti-counterfeiting, UV protector, multi-functional textile, optoelectronic devices, biological labeling, and damage detection for composite structures. The composite NFs have also exhibited some UV absorption capability through compromisation of optical transparency. This property has enabled the composite to function as UV shielding material through further research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean government (No. 2014R1A4A1008140), Ministry of Education, Science Technology (MEST) and National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) through the Human Resource Training Project for Regional Innovation (No. 201210A0404613010100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2014.12.061.
